talk_title: Integrating Epidemiology and Basic Science to Elucidate Prostate Cancer Inflammation, Telomere Biomarkers, Statin and Digoxin Repurposing

speaker.name: Elizabeth A. Platz
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Department of Epidemiology; Joint appointments in Oncology and Urology, Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine

targets.dependency_context: prostate cancer cells with high telomere length variability

biomarkers.name: Long‑term statin use
biomarkers.finding: associated with reduced risk of metastatic prostate cancer and prostate cancer death (HR approx. 0.5 in pooled analyses)

biomarkers.name: Long‑term digoxin use
biomarkers.finding: inverse association with organ‑confined and lethal prostate cancer; risk declines with longer duration

biomarkers.name: Telomere length variability in cancer cells + short stromal telomeres
biomarkers.assay: fluorescence in situ telomere length assay
biomarkers.finding: 14‑fold increased risk of prostate cancer death compared with low‑risk category

biomarkers.name: CMV seropositivity
biomarkers.finding: positively associated with greater percent area of intraprostatic inflammation

models.name: Health Professionals Follow‑up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: men with clinically localized prostate cancer, tissue microarrays available

models.name: Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial (PCPT)
models.genotype_or_key_features: end‑of‑study biopsies, finasteride vs placebo arms

models.name: SELECT trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: men from PCPT used as baseline for prospective inflammation analysis

models.name: ARIC cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: long‑term statin and cholesterol data, incident and fatal prostate cancer outcomes

clinical_data.n: n=~2,000 men in HPFS with tissue data
clinical_data.population: men with high telomere variability in cancer cells and short stromal telomeres

MOA_summary: Platz integrates population‑based epidemiology with pathology and molecular assays to show that intraprostatic inflammation, driven in part by infections such as CMV, increases prostate cancer risk; telomere length variability in cancer cells combined with short stromal telomeres predicts lethal disease; and long‑term use of statins or digoxin is inversely associated with aggressive prostate cancer, suggesting drug‑repurposing opportunities.
readouts: fluorescence in situ telomere length assay
key_findings: Intraprostatic inflammation measured in unbiased end‑of‑study biopsies is prospectively associated with increased prostate cancer risk (dose‑response with percent inflamed area).
CMV seropositivity is positively associated with greater intraprostatic inflammation in the PCPT cohort.
High variability of telomere length among cancer cells together with short stromal telomeres confers a 14‑fold higher risk of prostate cancer death, replicated at ~5‑fold in independent cohorts.
Long‑term statin use is linked to a ~50% reduction in metastatic prostate cancer and death, independent of cholesterol levels.
Long‑term digoxin use shows a similar inverse association with both organ‑confined and lethal prostate cancer.
Lower stromal telomere length correlates with reduced lamin B1 expression, supporting a senescent fibroblast hypothesis.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms link statin‑induced cholesterol lowering to reduced prostate cancer aggressiveness?
Can the telomere variability biomarker be translated into a clinically feasible assay?
What is the causal pathway by which digoxin reduces prostate cancer risk—ion‑transport inhibition or off‑target effects?
